source.title: Hydrogen Sulfide Release at Aghorn Operating Waterflood Station

actions_taken: Pumper A closed pump discharge valve and partially closed suction valve to isolate pump
Odessa Fire Rescue responded to the incident
Ector County Sheriff's Office responded to the incident
event.summary: On October 26, 2019, Aghorn Operating employee Pumper A responded to a pump oil level alarm at the Foster D waterflood station in Odessa, Texas. Without performing Lockout/Tagout, Pumper A worked to isolate Pump #1 by closing valves. The pump automatically re-energized, causing water containing H2S to release from a broken plunger. Pumper A was fatally exposed to H2S. Subsequently, his spouse gained access to the facility through unlocked gates, entered the pump house, and was also fatally exposed to H2S. Contributing factors included nonfunctioning H2S detection and alarm systems, inadequate ventilation of the pump house, absence of a safety management program, nonuse of personal H2S detectors, and deficient site security.
recommendations: Mandate use of personal H2S detection devices for all workers and visitors at waterflood stations where H2S concentrations may reach or exceed 10 ppm
Develop a site-specific, formalized and comprehensive Lockout/Tagout program including policies, procedures, and training compliant with 29 CFR 1910.147
Commission an independent and comprehensive analysis of each facility design regarding ventilation and mitigation systems
Develop and demonstrate a safety management program covering risk identification, assessment, mitigation, and monitoring compliant with 29 CFR 1910.1000 and 29 CFR 1910.147
Ensure H2S detection and alarm systems are properly maintained and configured with site-specific programs based on manufacturer specifications and industry good practice
Employ multiple layers of alerts (audible and visual) unique to H2S in detection and alarm system designs
Develop and implement a formal written site-specific security program to prevent unplanned entrance of non-employees, including locking access gates
OSHA to issue a safety information product addressing requirements for protecting workers from hazardous air contaminants and hazardous energy
Railroad Commission of Texas to issue a Notice to Operators describing the safety issues identified in this report
key_phrases: nonfunctioning H2S detection and alarm system

hazards.name: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) present in produced water at waterflood station

threats.name: Pump mechanical failure (broken plunger) causing loss of containment of produced water containing H2S
Automatic re-energisation of pump while worker performing maintenance without Lockout/Tagout

consequences.name: Fatal H2S exposure to worker (Pumper A) inside pump house
Fatal H2S exposure to non-employee (spouse of Pumper A) who accessed the facility
Accumulation of toxic H2S gas inside pump house due to inadequate ventilation

controls.name: Personal H2S Detection Device
controls.barrier_role: Detect elevated H2S concentrations and alert worker before fatal exposure
controls.lod_basis: First line of personal protection for worker entering H2S hazard area

supporting_text: Pumper A was not wearing his personal H2S detection device upon entering the waterflood station on the night of the incident
there is no evidence that Aghorn management required the use of these devices

controls.name: Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) Procedure
controls.barrier_role: Isolate pump from energy sources before maintenance to prevent automatic re-energisation
controls.lod_basis: Primary administrative control to prevent hazardous energy release during maintenance

supporting_text: At the time of the incident, Aghorn did not have any written Lockout / Tagout policies or procedures
Pumper A did not perform Lockout / Tagout to deenergize Pump #1 before performing work on it
The automatic activation of the pump allowed water containing H2S to release from the pump

controls.name: Fixed H2S Detection and Alarm System
controls.barrier_role: Detect H2S release and trigger alarms to warn personnel
controls.lod_basis: Secondary engineered detection layer to alert workers to H2S release

supporting_text: the H2S control panel did not receive signals from the internal and external detection sensors at the facility, and, therefore, did not trigger either of the two H2S alarms on the night of the incident
Nonfunctioning H2S Detection and Alarm System

controls.name: Pump House Ventilation System
controls.barrier_role: Dilute and remove H2S gas from pump house to reduce worker exposure concentrations
controls.lod_basis: First mitigation layer to reduce H2S concentration following release

supporting_text: The available ventilation methods did not adequately ventilate toxic H2S gas from the building during the incident, contributing to the high H2S levels to which Pumper A and his spouse were exposed
the CSB was unable to confirm whether the exhaust fans were operational at the time of the incident
the bay doors were approximately 60% open

controls.name: Site Security / Access Control
controls.barrier_role: Prevent unauthorised or unplanned access by non-employees to hazardous areas
controls.lod_basis: Secondary mitigation to limit exposure of non-employees to H2S hazard zone

supporting_text: As per Aghorn's informal policy, when an Aghorn employee is working at the facility, the access gates are normally left unlocked
The unlocked gates allowed Pumper A's spouse to drive directly to the waterflood station and enter the pump house, where she was exposed to toxic H2S gas

controls.name: Safety Management Program
controls.barrier_role: Provide overarching framework of policies, procedures, risk assessment, and training to prevent H2S incidents
controls.lod_basis: Organisational-level control underpinning all other barriers

supporting_text: Aghorn had no additional formal company safety or operational policies or procedures
Contributing to the incident was Aghorn's deficient safety management program

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
